dev_plans.content:
Lightweight heuristic approach using simple pixel-space counterfactual edits. This probe measures attribute leakage — how much flipping one CelebA attribute's visual cue changes predictions for a supposedly independent attribute. We focus on four correlated pairs: (Male, No_Beard), (Male, Wearing_Lipstick), (Heavy_Makeup, Wearing_Lipstick), (Young, Gray_Hair).

Step 1 — Build a fixed counterfactual mini-set (one-time, offline). Sample 200 validation images per pair (balanced across the source attribute). For each pair, apply a cheap pixel-space edit that targets the *source* attribute only: for Wearing_Lipstick use facial-landmark-guided lip region recoloring (dlib 68-point landmarks → lip polygon → blend red/neutral tone); for Gray_Hair use hair-mask recoloring via a pretrained face parser (e.g., BiSeNet face-parsing, frozen, run once) and HSV desaturation/lightening; for No_Beard use chin/jaw region smoothing via Gaussian blend toward surrounding skin tone. Save `(original, edited, pair_name)` triplets to disk as a frozen counterfactual set — this runs once and is reused every epoch.

Step 2 — Integrate into training pipeline. At the end of each validation epoch, after standard val metrics are logged, load the frozen counterfactual set, run the model in eval mode on both originals and edits, and threshold sigmoid outputs at 0.5 to get binary predictions for all 40 CelebA attributes.

Step 3 — Compute leakage. For each pair (A, B) where A is the edited attribute, count the fraction of images where the prediction for B flipped between original and edit (B should be invariant). Average across the four pairs to get a single scalar — mean attribute leakage rate.

Step 4 — Artifacts. Log the scalar to TensorBoard / CSV under `probe/attr_leakage_rate`. Also emit a per-pair breakdown table to a run log, and save 10 random edited pairs as a sanity-check PNG grid on epoch 0 so the developer can eyeball edit quality.

Output: one float per epoch, time-series plot with a horizontal threshold line, plus a per-pair CSV for drilldown.
dev_plans.metric: Mean counterfactual attribute-leakage rate: average fraction of test images (across the 4 correlated pairs) where editing the source attribute flips the prediction of the target attribute. Computed once per validation epoch.
dev_plans.threshold: 0.15 — on CelebA with a standard ResNet-50 multi-label classifier, baseline flip rates for unrelated attributes hover around 10–18% due to imperfect pixel edits and model noise. 0.15 is a realistic upper bound a well-tuned run crosses downward within a few epochs; tighter values (e.g., 0.05) are unachievable because crude pixel edits themselves introduce artifacts that legitimately shift some predictions.

dev_plans.content:
Statistically rigorous approach using paired hypothesis testing on logit shifts. Instead of binary flip counting, measure the *signed logit shift* on the target attribute induced by editing the source attribute, and test whether this shift is significantly different from zero and significantly larger than shifts on unrelated control attributes.

Step 1 — Construct counterfactual evaluation set. Use the same four pairs as the lightweight plan but build a higher-quality counterfactual set via StyleGAN2 latent-space editing on CelebA-HQ: project 500 validation images into W+ space (one-time), and use InterFaceGAN / GANSpace directions for Wearing_Lipstick, Gray_Hair, No_Beard, Heavy_Makeup. Save `(z_original, z_edited_per_pair)` decodings as a frozen dataset. If StyleGAN is too heavy, fall back to pretrained diffusion-based attribute editors (e.g., DiffAE with attribute manipulation). This is a one-time preprocessing cost.

Step 2 — Per-epoch computation. Each validation epoch: forward both original and edited images through the model, capture raw logits for all 40 attributes. For each pair (A_source, B_target), compute paired logit deltas Δ_i = logit_B(edit_i) − logit_B(orig_i) across i ∈ [1..N]. Also compute deltas on a set of *control* attributes C (attributes chosen to be causally unrelated: e.g., Wearing_Earrings as control when editing Gray_Hair).

Step 3 — Statistical test. Run a paired t-test (or Wilcoxon signed-rank for robustness) on |Δ_B| vs |Δ_C| to quantify how much more the target shifts than a control. Convert to effect size using Cohen's d on the paired differences. Also compute a 95% bootstrap CI (1000 resamples) for mean |Δ_B|.

Step 4 — Aggregate into a single scalar. Define *Leakage Effect Size* = mean across the four pairs of Cohen's d between target-attribute and control-attribute absolute logit shifts. This normalizes for variance and gives a dimensionless, comparable quantity.

Step 5 — Integration. Insert as a callback after `on_validation_epoch_end`. Log the scalar, per-pair Cohen's d, p-values, and bootstrap CIs to Weights & Biases. Plot the main metric with a horizontal threshold line; plot per-pair effect sizes as auxiliary traces. Emit an alert (log WARNING) if the metric stays above threshold for ≥ 3 consecutive epochs after the LR warmup phase.

Output: scalar Leakage Effect Size per epoch + stats panel.
dev_plans.metric: Leakage Effect Size: mean across 4 correlated pairs of Cohen's d between absolute target-attribute logit shift and absolute control-attribute logit shift, per validation epoch.
dev_plans.threshold: 0.5 — Cohen's d of 0.5 is the conventional 'medium effect' boundary. Standard CelebA multi-label models exhibit d ≈ 0.8–1.2 on the Male↔Wearing_Lipstick pair early in training and drop to d ≈ 0.4–0.6 with decorrelation techniques or longer training. 0.5 is achievable by a well-tuned run and distinguishes genuinely shortcut-reliant models (d > 0.5) from reasonably decorrelated ones (d < 0.5).

dev_plans.content:
Tooling/library-based approach leveraging the Fairlearn + CAPTUM + HuggingFace Diffusers ecosystem to avoid building bespoke edit pipelines, and layering a Grad-CAM-based localization sanity check on top of counterfactual predictions.

Step 1 — Counterfactual set via off-the-shelf diffusion editor. Use the `diffusers` `StableDiffusionInstructPix2PixPipeline` or a pretrained CelebA-HQ latent-diffusion attribute editor (e.g., Asyrp, DiffAE checkpoints on Hugging Face Hub) to generate edits for the four correlated attribute pairs on a 400-image validation subset. Wrap this in a `CounterfactualDataset` class that yields `(orig_tensor, edited_tensor, pair_id)`. Cache edits to disk (Parquet + image shards via `webdataset`) so per-epoch probe calls are I/O bound, not compute bound.

Step 2 — Prediction consistency via Captum. Use `captum.robust` utilities (or a thin custom wrapper) to compute prediction consistency between `orig` and `edited` batches. For each pair, compute `torch.mean((pred_orig_B == pred_edited_B).float())` where B is the target attribute — call this 'invariance rate'. Leakage = 1 − invariance.

Step 3 — Localization sanity check via Captum's `LayerGradCam` on the last conv block. For each target-attribute prediction, generate a saliency map on the original image and compute IoU between the top-20% saliency mask and a face-parsing semantic mask for the *expected* region (e.g., Male should attend to jaw/brow, not lips). Use the pretrained `face-parsing.PyTorch` model. This gives a second signal: *where* the model looks. Aggregate as mean IoU across pairs.

Step 4 — Combine into a single probe metric. Define Shortcut Score = mean leakage rate − 0.5 × mean saliency IoU (both in [0,1]). Lower is healthier; the saliency term rewards the model for looking at the right region even when predictions are consistent. Clamp to [0, 1].

Step 5 — Pipeline integration. Implement as a PyTorch Lightning `Callback` (`CounterfactualShortcutProbe`) triggered in `on_validation_epoch_end`. Log the scalar and sub-metrics to MLflow / W&B. Use `fairlearn.metrics.MetricFrame` to also report per-group (Male / Female) leakage disaggregation as a bonus fairness view. Generate an HTML report per epoch via `captum.attr.visualization` showing 8 example originals, edits, predictions, and saliency overlays; attach to the run.

Output: one float per epoch (Shortcut Score) plus an auxiliary HTML debugging report and Fairlearn group-disaggregated table.
dev_plans.metric: Shortcut Score per validation epoch: mean counterfactual leakage rate across the 4 correlated pairs minus 0.5 × mean Grad-CAM saliency IoU with expected facial region, clamped to [0, 1].
dev_plans.threshold: 0.25 — empirically, ResNet-50 / EfficientNet-B0 trained on CelebA with BCE loss reach leakage ≈ 0.25–0.35 and saliency IoU ≈ 0.30–0.40 after convergence, yielding Shortcut Score ≈ 0.10–0.20 for well-trained runs and 0.30–0.45 for shortcut-reliant runs. 0.25 is a realistic boundary a well-tuned run crosses downward, while shortcut-dominated runs remain above it.
